Title: Nitrogen Assimilation Plays a Role in Balancing the Chloroplastic Glutathione Redox Potential Under High Light Conditions

Common_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Serves as a chloroplast redox biosensor revealing that nitrogen assimilation-defective mutants fail to relax chloroplastic glutathione redox potential under high light.
Common_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Applied_Species: Constitutively expressed in transgenic lines selected for strong fluorescence
Common_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: Reflects chloroplast redox homeostasis and oxidative stress status under dynamic light
Common_Genes.Environment_or_Treatment_Factor: Normal light; medium light; high light; elevated CO2; H2O2; DTT
Common_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Chloroplast-targeted transgenic expression; confocal colocalization; ratiometric fluorescence imaging; automated plate-reader monitoring
Common_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. Chloroplast-targeted roGFP2 detected high-light-induced oxidation in WT and mutant plants. 2. WT plants showed oxidation followed by relaxation/reduction during or after high light, whereas gln2 and glu1 mutants lacked this relaxation under light. 3. Similar patterns under elevated CO2 supported a link to de novo nitrogen assimilation rather than photorespiration.
Common_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Useful as a diagnostic biosensor to phenotype redox consequences of nitrogen assimilation perturbation under fluctuating light, but not itself a breeding target for nutrient accumulation.
Common_Genes.Other_Important_Info: This is a reporter transgene, not an endogenous nutrient-metabolism gene; included because it is a core experimental protein used to quantify the metabolic redox phenotype.

Pathway_Genes.Pathway_Branch_or_Subpathway: Ammonium assimilation into glutamine in chloroplasts
Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: ATP-dependent assimilation of ammonium into glutamate to form glutamine.
Pathway_Genes.End_Product_Connection_Type: forms immediate precursor and directly assimilates inorganic nitrogen into amino acid metabolism
Pathway_Genes.Rate_Limiting_or_Key_Control_Step: Key control step supported; not explicitly established as rate-limiting
Pathway_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite: Glutamine; glutamate; chloroplastic glutathione redox potential
Pathway_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: Nitrogen assimilation products support amino acid biosynthesis and provide an electron sink that helps maintain photosynthetic efficiency and chloroplast redox balance under high light
Pathway_Genes.Environment_or_Treatment_Factor: Normal light; medium light; high light; darkness; elevated CO2
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Loss of GLN2 reduces 15N-nitrate incorporation into glutamine/glutamate, increases susceptibility of PSII to high-light photoinhibition, and abolishes normal high-light relaxation of chloroplastic EGSH.
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Mutant analysis (gln2 knockout line); immunoblot; 15N-nitrate isotope tracing with LC-HRMS; chlorophyll fluorescence (ΦPSII); chloroplast roGFP2 redox imaging; elevated CO2 comparison
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. gln2 plants had strongly reduced GS2 protein abundance. 2. gln2 showed significantly lower 15N incorporation into Gln and Glu than WT under normal and high light, confirming impaired de novo nitrate assimilation. 3. High light stimulated nitrate assimilation in WT, but this response was diminished in gln2. 4. gln2 plants displayed stronger PSII photoinhibition under high light than WT. 5. gln2 failed to show the WT-like relaxation of chloroplastic EGSH under high light, indicating GS2-dependent nitrogen assimilation helps dissipate excess reducing power.
Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Maintaining or enhancing chloroplastic GS2 activity may improve nitrogen assimilation efficiency, photoprotection, and redox stability under fluctuating/high light; useful target for engineering stress-resilient photosynthesis, with caution about integration with photorespiration and nitrogen supply.
Pathway_Genes.Applied_Promoters: Endogenous mutant allele; roGFP2 reporter lines used CaMV 35S only for the reporter
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: gln2 mutants remained viable and only slightly smaller than WT, unlike stronger GS2 mutant phenotypes reported in some other species. Residual nitrogen assimilation suggests partial compensation by cytosolic GS isoforms or related pathways.
Pathway_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Slightly smaller plants (~70% leaf area of WT); greater PSII vulnerability under high light when GLN2 is impaired.

Pathway_Genes.Pathway_Branch_or_Subpathway: Conversion of glutamine plus 2-oxoglutarate to glutamate in chloroplasts
Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Transfers the amide group of glutamine to 2-oxoglutarate, producing two glutamate molecules using reducing power from ferredoxin.
Pathway_Genes.End_Product_Connection_Type: directly forms target amino acid product and consumes photosynthetic reducing equivalents
Pathway_Genes.Rate_Limiting_or_Key_Control_Step: Key control step supported; central position in nitrogen metabolism, but not explicitly established as rate-limiting
Pathway_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite: Glutamate; glutamine; chloroplastic glutathione redox potential
Pathway_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: Supports amino acid biosynthesis and functions as a major electron sink helping sustain PSII efficiency and chloroplast redox homeostasis under high light
Pathway_Genes.Environment_or_Treatment_Factor: Normal light; medium light; high light; darkness; elevated CO2
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Loss of GLU1 strongly reduces 15N-nitrate assimilation into amino acids, causes chlorotic growth defects, enhances PSII photoinhibition under high light, and prevents WT-like chloroplastic EGSH relaxation.
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Mutant analysis (glu1 line); immunoblot; 15N-nitrate isotope tracing with LC-HRMS; chlorophyll fluorescence (ΦPSII); chloroplast roGFP2 redox imaging; elevated CO2 comparison
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. glu1 plants had strongly reduced GOGAT1 protein abundance. 2. glu1 showed markedly lower 15N incorporation into Gln and Glu than WT under all light regimes, confirming impaired nitrogen assimilation. 3. Unlike WT, glu1 did not show significant high-light stimulation of nitrate assimilation. 4. glu1 displayed chlorosis, reduced growth, and lower maximum and operational PSII efficiency. 5. glu1 lacked high-light relaxation of chloroplastic EGSH, supporting a role for GOGAT1-dependent nitrogen assimilation as an electron sink.
Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Enhancing chloroplastic Fd-GOGAT1 could strengthen nitrogen-use efficiency and photoprotection under high irradiance; however, severe growth defects in loss-of-function mutants indicate strong pleiotropy and need for balanced tuning rather than drastic perturbation.
Pathway_Genes.Applied_Promoters: Endogenous mutant allele; roGFP2 reporter lines used CaMV 35S only for the reporter
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: glu1 showed a typical photorespiratory phenotype in air, but similar redox/ΦPSII trends under elevated CO2 suggested the reported high-light redox phenotype mainly reflects de novo nitrogen assimilation rather than photorespiration. Residual assimilation may be partially compensated by GOGAT2 or NADH-GOGAT.
Pathway_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Severe growth reduction, chlorosis, and reduced PSII efficiency when GLU1 is impaired.
